problem_summary: 2025 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research / sustainable transportation), Team #2507692, Outstanding award. The task: model Baltimore's transportation network, quantify the impact of the Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse on diverse stakeholders, select and evaluate a transportation improvement project, propose a new project to maximally improve residents' lives, and address safety. The team built three linked models on a single graph-theoretic foundation: (I) an Adaptive Transportation Network Model to simulate the bridge collapse and its stakeholder impacts; (II) an Upgraded Bus Network Model using clustering to prioritize bus-shelter renovation/construction; (III) an Integrated Multimodal Transport Model that overlays bus and rail networks to recommend a new north-south rail line. The paper closes with a safety insight section, sensitivity analysis, and a mayor-addressed memorandum.
models_used: Graph-theoretic network model: city abstracted as a directed graph G=<V,E>, nodes from nodes_all.csv and edges from edges_all.csv, built/visualized in ArcGIS

Entropy Weight Method (EWM): objective edge-weighting from three factors (traffic flow, capacity, peak-time duration) via min-max standardization, proportion P_ij, entropy E_j = -k Sum P_ij ln P_ij with k=1/ln(n), weight W_j = (1-E_j)/Sum(1-E_j), then edge weight e_i = Sum W_j Z_ij

Flow-Balance Equation (conservation: Sum F_in = Sum F_out): collapse simulated by deleting the bridge edges and re-allocating its flow to the two remaining bridges proportionally to their capacity/importance
Dijkstra shortest-path algorithm: recompute shortest paths/distances d_ij after edge removal to quantify commuting-time changes for stakeholders
Comprehensive Ranking System: weighted score for bus stops, score = 0.6*ridership + 0.4*nonexistence_of_shelter (shelter present=0, absent=1)
Cluster Analysis: bus stops partitioned into 5 clusters by location + ridership (k=5, K-means-style)
Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) per cluster: connect all stops with minimum total edge weight to find key/edge connector stops; later perturbed for sensitivity test
Second shelter-quality score: score = 0.6*ridership + 0.2*central_latitude_proximity + 0.2*central_longitude_proximity (proximity to cluster geometric centroid)
Pearson correlation coefficient: between shelter score and number of bus routes through a stop, visualized as a heatmap
Integrated Multimodal Transport Model: ArcGIS overlay/buffer intersection (0.0015 buffer) of bus and rail networks to find coverage gaps; recommends a new north-south rail line
Data preprocessing pipeline: linear interpolation for missing ridership, IQR rule (Q1-1.5IQR, Q3+1.5IQR) for outlier removal, min-max normalization of ridership and coordinates
key_techniques: Single shared graph spine: one directed-graph abstraction reused across all three models so Model II is explicitly an optimization of Model I and Model III extends it — gives a coherent 'multi-model synergy' narrative
Objective weighting via entropy (EWM) rather than subjective AHP weights — lets the data dictate which of flow/capacity/peak-time matters most for each edge

Counterfactual simulation by edge deletion + flow re-allocation: physically removing the collapsed-bridge edges and redistributing flow, then re-running Dijkstra to measure before/after commuting time and AADT
Using MST not just to plan connectivity but as the object of the sensitivity test (perturbing leaf-node count and showing total edge length rises, arguing the original solution is optimal)
ArcGIS used end-to-end as the visualization and spatial-analysis engine: entropy-weighted choropleth of the road network, buffer-intersection overlay of bus vs rail to expose the north-south gap

Stakeholder decomposition repeated in every model: commuters (urban/suburban), merchants, logistics firms, transit riders, operators, planners, governments — directly answering the problem's multi-stakeholder framing

Two-stage shelter prioritization: first a ridership+shelter-absence urgency score to find where to act, then a centroid-proximity+ridership quality score to split actions into 'build new' vs 'renovate existing'
Validation of a correlation claim with Pearson + heatmap rather than asserting it qualitatively
Memorandum to the Mayor that translates the two technical projects into plain-language content/benefits/cons — satisfies the communication deliverable
why_it_won: From a judge's view, the paper wins on coherence, completeness, and communication rather than mathematical depth. It (1) addresses all four sub-problems plus the required memorandum, leaving no deliverable unanswered; (2) builds one unified graph-theory framework and layers three named, interlocking models on it, so the work reads as a single escalating story (simulate the shock -> optimize the bus layer -> integrate rail) instead of three disconnected exercises; (3) keeps the multi-stakeholder lens front and center in every section, which is exactly what an ICM policy problem rewards; (4) pairs each method with a real data pipeline (interpolation, IQR, normalization) and an objective weighting scheme (entropy) that signals rigor and reproducibility; (5) leans heavily on ArcGIS spatial visualization, giving the paper a professional, GIS-backed look that reads as 'real consulting'; and (6) closes the loop with sensitivity analysis, an explicit strengths/weaknesses section, and a clean mayor-facing memo. The judge sees a team that is organized, on-topic, data-literate, and communicative — the profile that earns Outstanding even when individual models are textbook-level.
section_highlights: Standout structural moves: a tight one-page Summary that names all three models and their roles; a clean Assumptions-with-Justifications table and a Notations table up front; per-model workflow flowcharts in 'Our Work' that preview the method chain (Graph Theory -> Flow Balance -> Dijkstra -> EWM). The before/after device is used repeatedly and effectively — paired 3D bubble charts and 3D bar charts of AADT pre/post collapse make the impact visually obvious. The two-equation shelter scoring (urgency score, then quality score) cleanly separates 'where' from 'what action', and the final shelter map (Figure 24) color-codes Build vs Rebuild vs existing stops. The rail/bus buffer-overlay figure visually justifies the single concrete recommendation (a north-south rail line marked in red). The paper ends with a Mayor-addressed Memorandum that re-expresses Projects 1 and 2 as Content/Benefits/Cons — strong audience-aware communication.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The rendered data tables/figures in the markdown are clearly synthetic or placeholder-looking (e.g., perfectly linear AADT 1000,1100,...,2100; bubble-chart latitudes of '4000'/'41O'; idealized grid-like cluster scatters), suggesting the visualizations may be illustrative mock-ups rather than outputs of real computation — a credibility risk a sharp judge could probe
Entropy weight method is applied but the actual computed weights W1,W2,W3 are never reported, so the 'data-driven' weighting is asserted but not shown
n is stated as 565496 edges yet no real computational results, runtimes, or aggregate statistics from Dijkstra over that graph are presented — the scale is claimed but not evidenced

The flow re-allocation rule ('proportional to capacity and importance') is vague and not formalized into equations; no congestion/BPR-type volume-delay function is used, so 'commuting time increased significantly' is qualitative
Cluster count k=5 is asserted with no justification (no elbow/silhouette analysis), and no clustering algorithm is explicitly named (presumably K-means)

Sensitivity analysis is weak: it only perturbs MST leaf-node count and notes total edge length rises — this tests MST optimality (trivially true) rather than the robustness of model outputs to input/weight assumptions
The shelter score mixes a normalized ridership in [0,1] with a binary shelter flag using fixed 0.6/0.4 weights that are unjustified and not sensitivity-tested
The safety sub-problem (Problem 4) is answered with qualitative policy bullets (maintenance, monitoring, innovative models) rather than any model, the thinnest section
Several assumptions (fixed capacity, constant travel behavior, negligible weather) are acknowledged but their impact on results is not quantified
reusable_patterns: One graph spine, many models: abstract the system as G=<V,E> once and frame every later model as an optimization/extension of it — yields automatic narrative cohesion in network/OR problems
Entropy Weight Method as a drop-in objective weighting for edge or alternative scoring when you have multiple incommensurable factors and want to avoid subjective AHP weights
Shock-as-edge-deletion + flow re-allocation + Dijkstra re-run: a clean recipe for any 'what if this link fails' resilience/disruption question
Two-stage scoring pattern: first a 'where is the need' urgency score, then a 'what action and how good' quality score, to convert a ranking into concrete build-vs-renovate decisions
Cluster -> MST -> connector-node selection: use clustering to segment, MST within clusters to expose backbone connections, and edge/connector nodes as intervention targets
Buffer-intersection overlay in GIS to expose coverage gaps between two networks (bus vs rail) and justify a single concrete infrastructure recommendation
Repeat a fixed stakeholder taxonomy (residents, merchants, logistics, operators, planners, government) in every model's impact subsection to directly satisfy multi-stakeholder ICM prompts
Standard data-cleaning preamble (linear interpolation for gaps, IQR for outliers, min-max normalization) presented as its own subsection to signal data rigor
Close with a stakeholder-addressed memorandum that restates each recommendation as Content/Benefits/Cons — strong template for the communication deliverable
Before/after paired visualizations (bubble charts, bar charts) as the primary device for communicating an intervention's effect
